title: Chapter 19: The View from Here
content: Chloe woke up on the couch because the couch was warm and the couch was safe and the couch had become her bed sometime around 3 AM when she'd migrated from Maya's room after a particularly loud thunder roll. The living room was washed in that specific morning gold that only hit the apartment between 7:42 and 8:15 — she knew this because she'd memorized the light's schedule, because capybaras were excellent at schedules, because the sun was the most reliable thing in her life now.
She lay there for a moment. Just breathing.

Her pulse was slow. Steady. The way it always was these days — 40 beats per minute, give or take, which was apparently absurd for a capybara but felt exactly right for her. She pressed a paw against her chest just to feel it. Thud. Thud. Thud. Alive. Real. Still here.

The apartment was quiet in the way it was only quiet on weekday mornings, when Maya hadn't yet started her music and Dave hadn't yet started his coffee grinder and Elif hadn't yet started her very existence. It was a good quiet. A held-breath quiet. The kind that didn't need filling.

Chloe stretched — a full-body capybara stretch that started at her nose and rippled down to her tail — and then she just lay there, watching the dust motes. They moved like tiny planets in the sunbeam. She could relate. Orbiting. No particular destination. Just moving because moving was what you did when the light caught you.
The scream started at 9:07 AM.

Chloe was mid-yawn when it hit — a sound so sharp and so joyful that she actually flinched, which for a 150-pound capybara was really more of a dignified wobble. She turned toward Maya's room, ears swiveling.

Maya appeared in the doorway. Her black bob was doing that thing where half of it was plastered to her face and the other half was achieving some kind of anti-gravity situation. Her eyes were red. She was holding her phone like it had personally saved her life.
"Chloe," Maya said. Her voice cracked. "Chloe. Chloe. Chloe."
Chloe waddled over because when someone says your name four times in that particular tone, you waddle.
Maya crouched down, held the phone out. The email was short. Almost boring in its bureaucratic plainness:
*Dear Ms. Reyes,*

*We are pleased to inform you that your submission has been accepted for the Harlow Gallery Spring Group Show, "Domestic Wilderness." The exhibition will run March 14–April 2. Please confirm your participation by—*

Maya read it out loud. Then she read it again. Then she read it a third time, each reading slightly more disbelieving than the last, like the words might rearrange themselves into something less wonderful if she didn't keep watching them.

"They said no last year," Maya whispered. "And the year before that. And — Chloe, this is the gallery. This is *the* gallery. This is the one where — do you remember when I showed you that Anaïs Mercier exhibition and I cried? That one. That's — I'm going to be in the same building where —"
She broke off. Not sad crying. The other kind. The kind where something you've been holding so tightly you forgot you were holding it finally lets go.

Chloe waddled the rest of the way over and put her head on Maya's knee. Just rested it there. Heavy and warm and present. Maya's hand found its way into the fur behind Chloe's ear, and they stayed like that for a while, Maya laughing and crying simultaneously, which was apparently a thing humans could do. Chloe filed that away. Another human mystery. Like tax brackets. Or jeans with no pockets.
The hallway celebration was not a quiet event.

Maya had gone to tell Dave — who was in his room, because Dave was always in his room when he wasn't in the living room, which was one of the many perks of being Dave — and the telling had immediately escalated into hallway pacing, hallway gesturing, and hallway dancing. Maya was dancing in the hallway. With a capybara. This was not an unusual sight in this particular hallway, but it was an enthusiastic one.

Maya had one hand on Chloe's back, the other still clutching her phone, and she was doing some kind of shimmy-step combination that might have been a dance or might have been a medical event. Chloe was waddling alongside her, matching her energy as best she could, which mostly meant walking in small circles and humming a low B-flat that made the hallway bulb flicker slightly.
"Get it, get it, get it—" Maya was chanting, spinning, laughing.
From Dave's room, a muffled: "Are we being robbed or celebrated?"
" harlow  GALLERY, DAVE!"
"Okay! Celebrated! Cool! Gonna put pants on!"
Across the hall and through the peephole, Mrs. Hendricks watched.

She'd been on her way to get the mail — she always got the mail at 9:15, because the mail came at 9:10 and she liked to give it a few minutes to settle — when she'd heard the scream. And then the laughter. And then the unmistakable sound of someone living loudly in a space that Mrs. Hendricks's apartment hadn't been in a very long time.

She pressed her eye to the peephole and watched Maya spin. Watched the capybara waddle in patient circles. Watched the way Maya's whole body was involved in the happiness, like she couldn't contain it in just her face so it had to spill out into her shoulders and her hands and her feet.
Mrs. Hendricks watched for a long time.

The hallway was quiet on her side of the door. Her apartment behind her was quiet too. The kind of quiet that had furniture and dust and six different types of silence: morning silence, afternoon silence, evening silence, TV-off silence, TV-on-but-not-really-watching silence, and the worst one — the silence that used to belong to someone else.

She watched until Maya finally stopped dancing and crouched down to hug the capybara, burying her face in that improbable fur, and something in Mrs. Hendricks's chest did a thing she didn't have a word for.
She stepped back from the door.
The apartment was exactly as she'd left it: neat, clean, perfectly maintained. A show home for a life that didn't have a second act.
She sat in her chair. She picked up her crossword. She set it down again.
She looked at the door.
Greg brought wine.

Not the cheap wine, either — Greg, who three months ago had been The Boss and was now just Greg, had a talent for showing up with the exact right thing at the exact right time, which was either emotional intelligence or a very well-calibrated sense of guilt. Probably both.

"This calls for the good stuff," he said, holding up the bottle like a trophy. "I don't even know what grape this is. The guy at the store said it was 'approachable but complex,' which I think is wine-speak for 'costs more than your first car.'"
"That was a very long way to say you overpaid," Dave said.
"I'm an overpayer, Dave. It's my brand."

They'd pushed the coffee table against the wall and spread out on the floor because Maya had declared that sitting on furniture was "for people who haven't just been accepted into the Harlow Gallery Group Show," and nobody had argued because you didn't argue with someone riding that particular high. Maya was on her stomach, feet kicked up behind her, sketching loose ideas for the show pieces while wine made her lines braver than they'd normally be. Dave was cross-legged, assembling a cheese plate with the focus of a surgeon and the portions of someone who'd forgotten to eat lunch. Elif was on FaceTime with someone, periodically holding the phone up so the person on the other end could see the capybara, which was doing Chloe's digital brand exactly zero favors but was apparently unavoidable.
Chloe sat between Maya and the cheese plate, which was her favorite position in any room configuration.

Greg was in the armchair. The armchair that used to be Greg's chair, back when Greg came over as The Boss and sat in the armchair because it was the seat of power in a room that otherwise had no power dynamics. He'd always sat there. It had been a whole thing.

Now Greg sat in the armchair and it was just a chair. He was just a person in a chair. The shape of him — broad shoulders, reading glasses perpetually pushed up on his forehead, that specific posture of someone who was used to taking up space and was trying to take up less — fit the chair the same way it always had. But the chair didn't mean what it used to mean.

Chloe looked at the empty chair and then at Greg in the chair and then at the empty space beside the chair where his briefcase used to go and she felt — not sad. Not exactly. Something thinner than sad. Nostalgic, maybe. She missed the shape of the ritual. The way Greg used to sit and Maya used to perch and Dave used to orbit and things had a pattern. Not the pattern itself — just the comfort of having one.

But the new pattern was good too. Greg in the chair with wine instead of a briefcase. Maya on the floor drawing instead of watching the clock. Nobody performing. Nobody managing. Just people and cheese and a capybara who was eyeballing the brie with professional interest.
"Chloe, I see you," Dave said, moving the cheese plate three inches to the left. "Use your words. Oh wait."
"She doesn't need words," Maya said, not looking up from her sketch. "She has a face that's an entire paragraph."
"She has a face that's a very calm threat to my brie."
Chloe hummed. Low. Warm. The note that made the wine taste better and the air feel softer and the apartment feel, for a moment, like — not a shelter. Not a hideout. Not the place they'd escaped to.
A home.
The difference was small. It was the difference between closing a door and opening one. Between filling a space and living in it. Between existing and being.

Mrs. Hendricks sat in her silent apartment across the hall, and Chloe hummed, and the walls held all of it — the laughter and the wine and the sketching and the quiet and the particular warmth of a Friday night with people who'd chosen each other — and none of it leaked out into the cold.
Not yet, anyway.

Chloe fell asleep sometime around eleven, full of cheese and warm with wine-adjacent fumes (Dave had spilled some near her and she'd inhaled opportunistically). She slept on the couch again. The couch was still the couch. The apartment was still the apartment.
But it felt different now. Like a word you've said a thousand times and suddenly hear clearly for the first time.
Home.
She dreamed of gold light and slow heartbeats and a door that was about to open.
